title: Parenting
subtitle: What the Bible Says About Parenting
summary: Scripture presents parenting as the stewardship of souls — a calling that requires instruction, modeling, discipline tempered with tenderness, and ultimately releasing children to walk their own path with God.
body:
The foundational text on parenting in Scripture is the Shema. Moses instructs Israel to teach God's commands diligently to their children — talking about them at home, on the road, at bedtime, and at sunrise [verse:deut.6.6-7]. Faith formation is not a weekly event but a lifestyle woven into ordinary moments.

Proverbs frames parenting as directional: train up a child in the way he should go, and when he is old he will not depart from it [verse:prov.22.6]. The Hebrew suggests training according to the child's own bent — not a one-size-fits-all approach but attentive, individualized guidance.

Paul balances authority with tenderness. Fathers are told not to provoke their children to anger but to bring them up in the discipline and instruction of the Lord [verse:eph.6.4]. The warning against provoking is as strong as the command to instruct — harsh parenting defeats its own purpose.

God himself models parenting. He disciplines those he loves, as a father the son in whom he delights [verse:prov.3.11-12]. The psalmist adds that God is compassionate toward his children as a father who knows their frame — who remembers they are dust [verse:ps.103.13-14].

Parenting in Scripture is not about producing perfect children. It is about faithful presence, honest instruction, and the humility to entrust outcomes to God.

verses.annotation: The Shema's parenting mandate: teach God's ways through the fabric of daily life.
Train according to a child's individual bent — attentive, personalized guidance.
Paul's dual command: do not provoke to anger; bring up in instruction and discipline.
God's discipline as a model — correction motivated by love, not control.
A father's compassion: God remembers that his children are dust.
Children are a heritage from the Lord — a gift to steward, not a project to manage.

scholars.quote: Craigie explains that Deuteronomy 6:6-7 envisions religious education not as formal instruction but as a continuous conversation — parents teaching through the natural rhythms of shared life rather than isolated lessons.
Longman reads Proverbs 22:6 not as an unconditional guarantee but as a wisdom observation — generally, early formation shapes lasting character, though Proverbs itself acknowledges exceptions.

Schreiner notes that Paul's warning against provoking children in Ephesians 6:4 was unusual in the ancient world, where paternal authority was virtually absolute — Paul places real limits on how fathers exercise power.
scholars.source: Paul, Apostle of God's Glory in Christ (Schreiner, 2001)
